Bhavsar a,b,⁎, Craig Fowler c, Sarah Day c, Steve Petro a, Nilima Gandhi b, Sarah B. Gewurtz b, Chunyan Hao a, Xiaoming Zhao a, Ken G. In this study, we conducted a detailed investigation of PFAA contamination in the Lake Accepted 23 May 2016 Niapenco area in Ontario, Canada, where among the highest ever reported levels of PFOS were recently measured Available online 11 June 2016 in amphipods, ﬁsh and snapping turtle plasma. Levels and distribution of PFAAs in water, sediment and ﬁsh sam- Keywords: ples collected from the area varied widely. An upstream pond beside a former ﬁreﬁghting training area (FFTA) Perﬂuoroalkyl acids PFAAs was conﬁrmed as the source of PFAAs even 20 years after the last use of the foam at the FFTA. Recent PFOS con- Perﬂuorooctane sulfonic acid PFOS centration in water (~60 ng/L) at Lake Niapenco, about 14 km downstream of the pond, was still 3–7× higher Fireﬁghting than the background levels. For PFOS, Log KD ranged 1.3–2.5 (mean ± SE: 1.7 ± 0.1), Log BAFs ranged 2.4–4.7 Guideline (3.4 ± 0.05), and Log BSAFs ranged 0.7–2.9 (1.7 ± 0.05). Some ﬁsh species-speciﬁc differences in BAF and Fish advisory BSAF were observed. At Log BAF of 4.7, ﬁsh PFOS levels at Lake Niapenco could reach 15,000 ng/g, 100× greater Worldwide than a “do not eat” advisory benchmark, without exceeding the current drinking water guideline of 300 ng/L. A ﬁsh consumption based water guideline was estimated at 1–15 ng/L, which is likely applicable worldwide given that the Log BAFs observed in this study were comparable to those previously reported in the literature. It appears that PFAA in the downstream waters increased between 2011 and 2015; however, further monitoring is required to conﬁrm this trend. Crown Copyright © 2016 Published by Elsevier Ltd. pdf_candidates.text_excerpt: w a t e r r e s e a r c h 4 7 ( 2 0 1 3 ) 6 9 9 2 e7 0 0 5 Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/watres Identification and characterization of metabolic properties of bacterial populations recovered from arsenic contaminated ground water of North East India (Assam) Soma Ghosh, Pinaki Sar* Department of Biotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, 721302 West Bengal, India article info abstract Article history: Diversity of culturable bacterial populations within the Arsenic (As) contaminated Received 4 April 2013 groundwater of North Eastern state (Assam) of India is studied. From nine As contaminated Received in revised form samples 89 bacterial strains are isolated. 16S rRNA gene sequence analysis reveals pre- 20 July 2013 dominance of Brevundimonas (35%) and Acidovorax (23%) along with Acinetobacter (10%), Accepted 17 August 2013 Pseudomonas (9%) and relatively less abundant (<5%) Undibacterium, Herbaspirillum, Rhodo- Available online 23 October 2013 coccus, Staphylococcus, Bosea, Bacillus, Ralstonia, Caulobacter and Rhizobiales members. High As(III) resistance (MTC 10e50 mM) is observed for the isolates obtained from As(III) Keywords: enrichment, particularly for 3 isolates of genus Brevundimonas (MTC 50 mM). In contrast, Arsenic contamination high resistance to As(V) (MTC as high as 550 mM) is present as a ubiquitous property, Assam irrespective of isolates’ enrichment condition. Bacterial genera affiliated to other groups Groundwater showed relatively lower degree of As resistance [MTCs of 15e20 mM As(III) and 250 Brevundimonas e350 mM As(V)]. As(V) reductase activity is detected in strains with high As(V) as well as Acidovorax As(III) resistance. A strong correlation could be established among isolates capable of Acinetobacter reductase activity and siderophore production as well as As(III) tolerance. A large number Biogeochemical cycling of isolates (nearly 50%) is capable of anaerobic respiration using alternate inorganic elec- tron acceptors [As(V), Se(VI), Fe(III), NO3 2 , SO4 2 , S2 O3 2 ]. Ability to utilize different carbon sources ranging from C2eC6 compounds along with some complex sugars is also observed. Particularly, a number of strains is found to possess ability to grow chemolithotrophically using As(III) as the electron donor. The study reports for the first time the identity and metabolic abilities of bacteria in As contaminated ground water of North East India, useful to elucidate the microbial role in influencing mobilization of As in the region. ª 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction region (GBM delta) of India and Bangladesh (Fendorf et al., 2010). Brahmaputra plain, covered by young alluvial sedi- As contamination in groundwater from natural sources is ments with Holocene sedimentary aquifers enriched with As, affecting the health of over hundreds of millions of people share similar sedimentological history with Bangladesh worldwide, most notably in Ganga Brahmaputra Meghna delta (Enmark and Nordborg, 2007). pdf_candidates.text_excerpt: Environ Dev Sustain (2017) 19:1015–1040 DOI 10.1007/s10668-016-9786-7 A study on the arsenic concentration in groundwater of a coastal aquifer in south-east India: an integrated approach S. Chidambaram1 • R. Thilagavathi1 • C. Thivya1 • U. Karmegam1 • M. V. Prasanna2 • AL. Ramanathan3 • K. Tirumalesh4 • P. Sasidhar5 Received: 15 August 2015 / Accepted: 31 March 2016 / Published online: 12 April 2016  Springer Science+Business Media Dordrecht 2016 Abstract The occurrence of arsenic in drinking water and its detrimental effects have drawn much attention in recent years. Several studies have been conducted in the deltaic plains of River Ganga, NE part of the India, and in other countries, but no systematic study was conducted in South India on occurrence of arsenic in groundwater. The main aim of this study is to determine the level of arsenic in groundwater and to understand the relation with other geochemical parameters of groundwater in the south-eastern coastal aquifer at Kalpakkam region, India. This region is represented by three different lithologies, viz. charnockites, flood plain alluvium and marine alluvium. Twenty-nine representative samples of groundwater were collected and analysed for major ions, metals and isotopes such as 2H and 18O. In addition, geophysical method was also attempted to understand the subsurface condition. The spatial variation in arsenic (As) indicates that higher concen- tration was observed around the landfill sites and irrigated regions, which was supported by geochemical, statistical and isotopic inferences. The variation in the As with depth, lithology and sources has been clearly brought out. Though the values of As does not exceed the drinking water permissible limit (10 mg/l), it has reached a near permissible level of 8.7 ppb. Hence, it is essential to understand the geochemical behaviour of As for a proper future management of the water resource in the study area. Keywords Arsenic  Groundwater  Geochemistry  Isotopes  South-east India & M. V. Prasanna geoprasanna@gmail.com 1 Department of Earth sciences, Annamalai University, Annamalai Nagar 608002, India 2 Department of Applied Geology, Faculty of Engineering and Science, Curtin University, CDT 250, 98009 Miri, Sarawak, Malaysia 3 School of Environmental Sciences, JNU, New Delhi, India 4 Isotope Applications Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Mumbai 400 085, India 5 Safety Research Institute, IGCAR, Kalpakkam, India 123 1016 S. Chidambaram et al. 1 Introduction The increasing requirement of the need for domestic usage of water led to the exploration and extraction of groundwater resources apart from surface water supplies. This extraction of groundwater resulted in overdrafting, land subsidence, and finally led groundwater quality to be unsuitable for human consumption. It is observed by researchers that the enhanced usage of groundwater resources in countries such as India and Bangladesh has resulted in health impacts on human (Bagla and Kaiser 1996; Kumar 1997; Nickson et al. 1998). It was inferred that about 36 million people are under the hazard of drinking arsenic contaminated in the Bengal Delta. Arsenic being the twentieth most abundant element, it is observed in all the components of the ecosystem such as atmosphere, lithosphere, hydrosphere and biosphere. Arsenic in groundwater is mainly due to the dissolution of ores and minerals in elevated areas due to bed erosion. It may also be contributed by industrial effluents in some areas (Dubey et al. 2012; Oinam et al. 2011). Commercially they are also used as wood preservatives, alloying agents, etc. The release of arsenic to the environment is mainly through combustion of fossil fuels. The health impact on the public is mainly through consumption of water and exposure to hazardous occupation (WHO 2001). The concentration, chemistry source and the variations in arsenic have been studied by different authors (Table 1). It is evident from Table 1 that efforts were undertaken worldwide, but little attention was paid to natural background values in rocks, soils and waters at local scales and this study is one such attempt on groundwater. The present study area has been investigated for different aspects of hydrogeochemistry: quality (Karmegam et al. 2010; Chidambaram et al. 2011), mixing proportion of groundwater (Karmegam et al. 2011), Saturation Index (SI) of clay minerals (Chidambaram et al. 2011) and heavy metal con- centration in groundwater (Chidambaram et al. 2012). The understanding of groundwater system also requires knowledge of stable isotope behaviour in a multifaceted approach (Vengosh et al. 2002; Widory et al. 2004) in order to understand various geochemical controls. In this paper, an attempt has been made to analyse the concentration of arsenic in groundwater of this region, their spatial variation, geochemical nature and source by an integrated approach, using stable isotopes and statistical techniques. 2 Study area The area undertaken for study, Kalpakkam in the Kanchepuram district, comprising hard rock and sedimentary formations located in northern part of Tamil Nadu between 12370 and 12250 north latitudes, 80000 and 80120 east longitudes (Fig. 1). The study area falls in Survey of India toposheet Nos. 66 D 2, 3 and 4 with a total area of 385 km2 bounded in east by Bay of Bengal. The SADRAS backwater is also located in the eastern part along the coast. The landfill sites for the Mahabalipuram Township are also located along the NE. The fluviomarine alluvium and the charnockites are the major litho units. The main river that drains the region is Palar dividing the study area dominated by charnockites into two as southern and northern. The quaternary fluvial alluvium is noted along the river course as it diagonally flows along north-west to south-east. Spatially, marine alluvium is repre- sented along the coast (Fig. 1). Singh et al. (2004) and Singh and Saxena (2004) report that the charnockites form the Archae
